purpose: Implement the runtime core needed for real governed agent execution: WO parser, workflow grammar, execution plan, audit memory, governed-run CLI.
files_modified: Makefile (added work-order-parser-check, workflow-grammar-check, execution-plan-check, audit-memory-check, governed-run-check; NOT in ci yet)
what_is_now_real: Markdown work-order parser that rejects: missing objective, missing stop, empty input, protected-and-mutable contradiction without explicit permission, placeholder-only deliverables.
Workflow grammar with 8 stages and ordering enforcement: execute-after-review, verify-after-execute (unless verifying existing artifact), report-before-stop, stop/refuse terminal.
Execution-plan validator that refuses: forbidden-path touch, missing required command, unbounded shell, destructive command without permission, plan without verify, plan without report.
Append-only audit-memory JSONL with SHA-256 hash chain and four-status verification: VALID, TAMPERED, EMPTY, INVALID.
governed-run CLI subcommand: parse WO → build plan → validate → audit-append → emit manifest JSON → final status (VALID / REFUSED / INVALID).
5 Makefile targets (work-order-parser-check, workflow-grammar-check, execution-plan-check, audit-memory-check, governed-run-check); NOT in `make ci` per WO 015.
what_remains_unimplemented: Actual shell execution: governed-run is dry-run-only in v0.1.0. Wiring the validated plan into a real sandboxed executor remains. Existing tools/pipeline_runtime.py already provides the proposer→review→executor pipeline; integrating governed-run with that pipeline is a follow-up.

Persistent audit-memory across CLI invocations: append_event/verify_chain support persistence, but the governed-run CLI invocation creates a new file each --audit run; multi-run aggregation patterns are not yet exercised by tests.

Workflow-stage parsing in arbitrary WO formats: parse_workflow_from_text uses a keyword heuristic. WOs that bury stages inside deeply nested prose may not be parsed correctly. The validator never silently accepts an invalid stage list — bad parses surface as 'plan has no verification step' or similar.

Refused-but-actionable handling: when validate() returns violations, the CLI emits GOVERNED_RUN_REFUSED and the audit log records the reasons. A separate sealed RefusalRecord artifact in the format defined by intellagent_runtime/refusal.py is NOT yet produced — that integration is a follow-up.

Path-glob semantics for forbidden paths: the implementation uses literal-substring + regex matching, which is conservative (false positives are possible, false negatives less so). True glob expansion via fnmatch is a follow-up.

Audit-memory locking under concurrent appends: file-locking is not implemented. Concurrent append_event calls from multiple processes against the same file may interleave; verify_chain would detect the resulting corruption but reordering is not prevented.
risks_introduced: The work-order parser is heuristic (regex-based, not a formal grammar). A WO that uses unusual heading syntax may yield incomplete fields. Mitigation: every required field rejected when absent; parser is conservative.

Destructive-command detection is heuristic. A novel command form not on the deny-list could pass. Mitigation: explicit allow-list approach in the execution surface remains the strongest backstop; this validator is one of several layers.
Hash-chained audit log is single-writer. Multi-writer use without external coordination will corrupt the chain. Mitigation: verify_chain reliably detects corruption.

governed-run does not yet integrate with the existing pipeline_runtime sandbox. Calling --dry-run is safe; calling without --dry-run currently does NOT execute commands either (it just emits the manifest). This is deliberate for v0.1.0; the runtime kernel and execution dispatch remain decoupled.

decisions.decision: audit-memory hash format: prefixed (sha256:<hex>) vs bare hex.
decisions.why: Consistent with the rest of the WiseOrder repo (Class A artifacts, manifests, vectors_suite_sha256). Single-format across the codebase reduces audit-side confusion.
decisions.cost_if_wrong: low; only the audit log is affected; a future migration would be a simple per-line replace.

decisions.decision: governed-run without --dry-run still does not execute shell commands.
decisions.why: v0.1.0 keeps the governance kernel decoupled from execution dispatch. tools/pipeline_runtime.py is the existing executor surface; wiring governed-run into it is a follow-up that deserves its own work order so the integration is reviewable.
decisions.cost_if_wrong: low; --dry-run is the safer mode and is the only one tested.

decisions.decision: Workflow-stage parsing uses a keyword heuristic, not a formal grammar.
decisions.why: WOs are author-written prose, not machine-generated. A heuristic that recognizes 90% of WOs is more useful than a strict grammar that recognizes 30% and rejects the rest as malformed. The validator catches the consequences of any parse failure (no verify / no report).
decisions.cost_if_wrong: medium; a misparsed WO becomes a refused plan rather than an unsafe execution.

next_build_task: Integrate governed-run with tools/pipeline_runtime.py so a GOVERNED_RUN_VALID plan flows into the existing proposer→review→executor pipeline under macOS sandbox-exec isolation. Acceptance criteria: a valid WO can be dry-run AND wet-run (with explicit --execute flag), execution stays within the planned commands, audit memory captures pipeline_hash + proposer_hash + review_hash + executor_manifest_hash, and refusal at any pipeline stage seals a RefusalRecord using intellagent_runtime/refusal.py.
